TextENGNew: By now, Hanyuu had completely settled into our circle.
Perhaps because she was close in age to Rika-chan and Satoko, she seemed to fit in with them especially well.
Of course, Rena was never going to leave her alone, and Mion was never going to ignore a new friend either.
I found myself thinking back to what Rena had told me before.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: How Hanyuu had actually been somewhere nearby all along, watching us, always wanting to join in.
...And yet I really couldn't recall ever seeing Hanyuu's face before...
...But somehow, the idea that she might have wanted to be part of our circle — I found I could believe that without difficulty.
changeReason: New translation adds substantial detail absent from the original, significantly expanding the information conveyed.

TextENGNew: Everything that should have surprised me when I first encountered it, Hanyuu accepted as though she had always known it.

The strictest rules of the club, the hours that slipped away while we played until we forgot about sundown — all of it she seemed to know inside and out,@k ...and she looked like someone savouring the joy of finally being able to take part.
changeReason: New translation expands on the original with additional detail that alters reader understanding.

TextENGNew: "...Hanyuu has completely become one of us now."
``Yeah. It feels like she's been here all along, somehow. Which shouldn't be the case at all.``
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: "...It isn't strange at all. She has always been by our side."
``Oh, you say that too, Rika-chan. ...Rena said the same thing. Maybe I'm the only one who never noticed.``
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: "...Keiichi is dense, so you certainly wouldn't have noticed. Mii~☆"
"Che. Fine, I'm dense, I know."
"Au au au. Rika and Keiichi are just sitting there. Are you tired?"
``...Hanyuu is simply tireless. Rika and I are quite worn out from playing too much.``
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: "But I've wanted to play with everyone for such a long, long time — this isn't nearly enough for me. I want to play more and more. Au au au☆"
"Hahaha, you've got so much energy. ...Say, Hanyuu — that thing on your head. Does it fall off when you're running around?"
Hanyuu pressed her hands to the horns on her head.
changeReason: Description changes from a state to an action (or vice versa), depicting a different scene.

TextENGNew: I had thought of them as a kind of accessory of hers, and it occurred to me it would be a shame if they fell off during rough play — I was thinking I could hold onto them for her.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: But Hanyuu kept her hands on her horns and looked at me with a troubled, apologetic expression.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: "...Keiichi. Hanyuu's cannot be removed."
"Eh? Oh — is that so...? Ahaha, sorry for saying something weird."
"...Au... does it look strange, after all... having horns like this..."
"Ahahahaha, it doesn't look strange at all, it's cute and adorable~"
``Exactly! If that counts as strange, then Rena's something else entirely — spot something cute and her tongue starts stretching out, her neck goes all unyoooon... guheh?!``
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: ``H-hauu~!! Don't describe me like some kind of yokai~!``
changeReason: Cultural term changed from 'monster' to 'yokai', carrying different cultural connotations.

TextENGNew: "...Rena-san's punch still comes out of nowhere, as always. Are you all right, Mion-san? Your face is caved in."
Everyone burst out laughing, but Hanyuu alone couldn't manage to laugh — her expression was ambiguous, strained.
changeReason: Translation differs significantly in the amount of detail conveyed.

TextENGNew: ...I probably said the wrong thing, I thought.
She probably didn't much like having her horns pointed out.
But trying to apologise in some awkward way might only make things worse...
I was still casting about for the right words when Rena invited the girls to continue playing, and they all headed back toward the torii.
changeReason: Translation differs significantly in the amount of detail conveyed.

TextENGNew: "...Don't worry about it, Keiichi. ...Hanyuu tries not to let it bother her too, but she still needs more practice at that. Mii."
"So she was bothered. Sorry — I'll be more careful from now on."
"...Hanyuu came to this Hinamizawa long, long ago."
"Ah, so I was right? That makes sense — she knew a lot about the village for a transfer student."
"......Where Hanyuu lived before she came to Hinamizawa, I don't really know."
I was told, but... I can only describe it as somewhere beyond my understanding.
There, Hanyuu — or rather, Hanyuu's people — had come to a point where they could no longer live.
And so they had travelled for a very long time, searching for a place where they could dwell, and eventually found their way to this land.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: It was a clear, beautiful marsh — and the destination that Hanyuu's people had sought without ceasing.
...But the land was already a village, and there were people living there who had been there first.
To revere those who came before was the one law of Hanyuu's wandering people.
Hanyuu's people chose to coexist with the villagers in peace.
But... coexistence did not go well.
The villagers were not yet mature enough, not yet grown enough, to accept the new arrivals.
...Their fierce, fierce rejection brought about a sad and terrible outcome.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: It was to settle this that Hanyuu descended to this place in a form modelled after them.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: But for some reason, her horns were the one thing she could not conceal.
changeReason: Rephrasing changes the emphasis or detail in a way that could affect reader interpretation.

TextENGNew: The forbidden ancient texts tell that she descended to the Furude Shrine in the form Hanyuu wears today.
She appealed that Hanyuu's wandering people could not survive anywhere other than this village,
and that they could only survive through coexistence with its people.

The head priest of the Furude Shrine at first refused, saying that if the wandering people's blood mingling with the villagers' drove those people to be possessed by demons, that could not be called coexistence.
But... the young heir of the Furude Shrine took a liking to Hanyuu, and when she bore him a child, the head priest was forced to reconsider.
If it is the wish of you wandering people with your hidden horns to remain in this land and within this bloodline, we shall consider fulfilling it.
But the villagers, contaminated by the demon's blood, could no longer tell human from demon. The villagers suspected one another, and were gripped by mutual distrust.
Unless that can be dispelled, no path to coexistence can be opened.
...For us to coexist with you, there are certain laws that must be kept.
They are very strict, and I feel deeply sorry for imposing them on you who have lived here peacefully until now.
But if you will honour these laws, the village will be freed from the curse of the demons.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: Very well — we shall honour those laws.
...But the village had already spilled much blood.
The people's anger, their sins, their defilement could not be cleansed.
In the human world, the only law is that sin is atoned by life.
I see.
If it is the custom of your world that someone must bear all blame and be buried with the sin, then I shall follow that custom.
But in that case, will not everyone, fearing for their lives, deny their sins, and this become an endless passing of blame forever...?
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ...Indeed.
That is the very demon of ugliness in the human world.
...No one acknowledges their own sins; they push them onto one another.
Because they can only be settled at the cost of one's own life, everyone refuses to settle, and sins only pile up without end.
Unsettled, festering, endlessly pushed from one to another — people suspecting and blaming each other...
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: I see. If that is this world's inescapable law and the law that troubles you, then in exchange for coexistence, I will give you a world where people need not push their sins onto one another.
...What kind of world would that be?
As long as there is a human world, sin will surely arise, and defilement will be born.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: Can you truly make it vanish without laying it on anyone?
Lay all the sins and defilement born from human beings entirely upon me.
And then drive me away.
changeReason: Ritual action changed from 'purify' to 'drive away', which are conceptually different.

TextENGNew: All sin does not reside in people.
If it is laid upon me, people will no longer push their sins onto one another.
What are you saying?
...Are you saying you will become a pillar-sacrifice?
It would also accord with the law of your world — that someone must bear responsibility and thus provide settlement.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ...Another ancient text tells it differently.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ...This, too, is a forbidden text.
Not even the three families may touch it — only the head of the Furude household.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: It says that the hearts of the people grew troubled, the villagers' wicked hearts accumulated in the marsh, and from it demons rose up.
The head priest of the Furude Shrine argued that the root of all evil troubling the people's hearts was the demon-god that had appeared from the marsh, and led the village's young men toward it.

At the water's edge stood the demon-god, mocking the turmoil in the people's hearts. The young men bravely challenged it, and though they took wounds, they brought it down, captured it, and dragged it to the Furude Shrine.

The head priest performed a ritual of exorcism on the demon-god, then cut open its belly, pulled out its entrails, tore them away and cast them into the stream. The body was sunk and buried in the marsh.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: And immediately the turmoil in the people's hearts grew calm. The people who had been suspecting and reviling one another joined hands, and rejoiced together in the peace that had arrived.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: ......In other words, if the forbidden ancient texts are true —
The one we villagers revere as Oyashiro-sama was... seized by our own hands, made to bear all sin, and torn to death.
Moreover, the texts state clearly that the demons appeared after the people's hearts had grown troubled.
In other words... the demons were made to carry the sins of human discord that had already existed before they even appeared.
Oyashiro-sama is a god who bears sin in humanity's place, and forgives conflict between people.
That is what the texts say eventually came to be revered, and became the origin of the Oyashiro-sama faith.
...I don't understand what all of this means.
...But there is one thing I can understand.
It is that she was a being who drew, on humanity's behalf, the short straw that no one else would draw.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: The evidence is this — just as the Watanagashi is today, we press the defilement clinging to our bodies into the cotton, and float it down the stream as our purification rite.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: In that process, there is no ugliness, no passing of blame.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: Before that system was born, we were demons — beings who pushed defilement and responsibility onto one another.
Making it someone's fault, offering someone as a sacrifice, tearing open someone's belly — that was the only way to settle the sin that had occurred.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: But it was not a human who bore it. It was Hanyuu.
A purification rite that required no human life.
A system capable of forgiving human sin.
The origin of the Oyashiro-sama faith: seeking responsibility for human sin not in humans, but in a divine curse.
These texts were sealed away, deep and deeper.
Because otherwise... it would become known that the being we are supposed to revere was actually one onto whom we pushed all our sins and buried.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: ...Hanyuu apologises.
She always takes upon herself the sins of people, and continues to apologise.
When a person can no longer bear the weight of their own sin, she takes it on.
Because Hanyuu takes the sin upon herself, the person's sin is cleansed, and that person is forgiven the right to keep living.
......The demon, drawing all sin into itself like cotton, absorbing it — is destroyed and cast into the stream, and the village is forgiven.
From the sins it generated itself.
If that is true... what a sorrowful existence Hanyuu — no, Oyashiro-sama — truly is.
I once said that to Hanyuu.
And she told me a story I felt I had heard somewhere before.
......The story of four people shut inside a mountain hut in the snow, sitting at the four corners and playing a message-relay game.
It was a freezing mountain hut.
As they were, they would fall asleep.
If they slept, they would die.
So someone proposed this.
Since they were each sitting at one of the four corners, the first person would walk clockwise to the next corner and wake the person sitting there.
The person woken would sit in that corner, while the person who had just been woken would stand up, walk clockwise to the next corner, and wake the person there in turn.
...If they kept repeating this in a circle, no one would fall asleep before morning — or so the logic went.
Those who know the twist in this story will need no explanation, but... this method doesn't work with four people.
The corner vacated by the first person to stand up and walk becomes empty.
So when they go around the full circle, when the fourth person goes to the next corner, it is empty.
...And he sits down there and falls asleep, which breaks the relay, and they all freeze to death.
In other words, four people aren't enough.
Without one more person, they cannot be saved.
This story is often told as a ghost story.

...The twist is usually that they were actually a party of five, one of whom had already frozen to death, and whose spirit joined the game — which was why they were able to keep the relay going until morning.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ......Hanyuu once told me that she is something like that fifth person.
If her joining the circle of human beings, just one more presence, could make everything turn smoothly —
It felt to her like a very apt comparison to the mountain-hut message relay working, going round and round in a circle.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: In the gap of a circle that humans alone cannot complete, a presence like Hanyuu steps in and makes the human circle whole.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ...And by doing so, all people can live gently.
A presence that makes it possible — that fills the gap in the broken circle.
...Was it because being called a demon and made to bear all sin and be slain summoned memories that she was so sensitive about her horns?
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: Did some unconscious instinct that a horned being is a being to be persecuted still prickle and torment her?
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: ``...Those horns are something she was born with. She was despised for them, and there were times when she was bullied.``
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: "S-is that right...?! ...That makes it even worse that I said what I said..."
``And... because of those horns, Hanyuu took upon herself the very great duty of receiving all sin and providing settlement for it... she carried it out... and was revered as a god.``
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: "Huh? A god??"
"...It is just my private musing. You may forget it."
Who Hanyuu truly is... even I don't fully understand.
Whether she is a divine being who descended from a dimension far above the human world.
Or whether she was simply an unfortunate girl who was sacrificed for no reason other than that she was born with horns.
I don't know.
But that girl's blood mingled with the blood of the Furude family, and has been passed down through generations to reach me.
...The legend that if the Furude family is blessed with a daughter as the first child for eight consecutive generations, that child is a reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama.
What this means, even I don't fully understand.
What does it mean that I am that eighth generation... and that only I could see Hanyuu?
A span of time stretching back more than eight generations — dizzyingly vast.
changeReason: Old simplifies 気の遠くなるような (dizzying/mind-boggling) to merely 'very long'; new captures the emotional weight of the Japanese phrasing.

TextENGNew: ...It was probably the time needed for the sins and defilement that Hanyuu took upon herself to be cleansed.
Eight is a number that spreads at its ends — 八 —
a very auspicious number.
There is a curse that goes "I will haunt you for seven generations," but eight surpasses that.
...In other words, it is an auspicious number that can forgive even a sin or curse that extends across seven generations.
It is a number of charm and incantation.
...The time needed to cleanse the sin that Hanyuu bore in humanity's place.
...What Hanyuu's people wished for was coexistence.
And yet, for that very purpose, she became a pillar-sacrifice, and Hanyuu alone, belly torn open along with all the defilement, was cast into the stream and sunk in the marsh.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: And so... the Furude family had been waiting, for so, so long.
Through a span of time beyond imagining, they had kept the tradition, and waited — waited for the sin that Hanyuu had cradled and slept with to be cleansed, and for her to return.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: After all... if tradition is to be believed, the character 古 in the name Furude came from taking the character for fortune-telling, 占, and adding the horn of a demon to make it 古.
The Furude family was founded at the moment Hanyuu's blood was mixed into it.
Hanyuu is the ancestor of the Furude family, and... the mother of us all.
From early childhood, Hanyuu taught me more than my own mother ever could.
...And no wonder.
I am, after all... Hanyuu's descendant.
The curse of a lineage that despised and buried its own mother — atoned through eight generations, breaking the curse that had seemed to stretch without end.
And... in the final generation, when the Furude family was on the verge of extinction, she came face to face with me, the last of her line.
When I think of this... sometimes I feel that this strange fate laid upon me holds some important meaning.
What meaning does the ordeal of breaking through the obstacles that seal my fate hold for the curse that may stretch more than a thousand years between Hanyuu and the Furude family?
...Though it must be said — a thousand years was too long a time to transmit those wishes and feelings with accuracy.
Hanyuu's existence was turned into the vengeful deity Oyashiro-sama, and the being who forgave all was, without anyone noticing, transformed into a being that bound all.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: The rules for coexistence were twisted into rules that bound the villagers, and they remained bound by them until the Meiji era.

And then once again, amid the winds of enclosure and misunderstanding set in motion by the dam conflict, Hanyuu and I encountered each other — and what meaning is there in the strange fate that closed in on us?
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ......My life is too short to understand that.
One thing can be said: the Furude family, which began when Hanyuu's blood was mixed into it, in its final generation, was able to meet once more with its ancestor.
The blood of the Furude family is the history of a curse — of making their mother bear all human sin and burying her.
Its beginning and its end... meet.
Hanyuu, who had always, always wanted to join everyone's circle.
But whose horns she could never hide.
She who, even so, wished to be the presence that filled the gap in the broken circle.
To bury one's parent is the duty of a child.
But, Mother.
I have never heard that a child must kill and bury their parent.
Do not ask, my child.
I am not human.
To bear horns is the mark of a demon. The demon's duty is to carry the calamities of the human world.
Therefore it is my purpose — my calling — to take upon myself the disasters of a troubled world and cleanse, in my own body, the discord in people's hearts.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: Why must the sins of the human world be laid on you alone, Mother?
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: Human sins are human sins.
They are not your sins, Mother.
Listen, my child.
People live on, drowning in sin.
They cannot survive without pushing it onto someone else.
Because they do not want to be the one it is pushed onto, they push it onto each other.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: That is the demon — the true demon that disorders the human world.
If I bear it, and people drive me away, people will be freed from the destiny of doubting and warring against one another.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: All sins and defilement, all karma and curse — lay them upon me.
And then strike me down, drive me away, cast me into the stream, and sink me in the marsh.
changeReason: Ritual action changed from 'purify' to 'drive away', which are conceptually different.

TextENGNew: This must not fall to a human being.
It is the fate of one who is not human to take it on.
If it is laid upon a human, people will not be freed from the demon of suspicion.
I do not understand.
I do not understand, Mother.
Mother truly has horns that cannot be hidden.
But can horns alone make someone not human?
Even if one is human — if one has horns, does that make one not human?
Whatever horns Mother may have, to me, Mother is nothing other than human.
My child, you alone say this to me...
Even if all others call me ******, ******, you alone call me human...
...What do the many forbidden texts of the Furude family — offered at the altar of the ritual storehouse, their existence unknown even to the heads of the other three families — tell us?
To understand them... would require a span of time as long as the time I have spent repeating these cycles.
......And if that understanding were reached... the smile on Hanyuu's face as she plays carefree with everyone might become even more legible...
"Ahhhhh!! A-are you all right?!"
Mion's startled cry reached me from somewhere.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: From the tone of her voice, something had gone wrong.
changeReason: Translation differs significantly in the amount of detail conveyed.

TextENGNew: "Wh-what?! What happened?!"
"Hanyuu-san was fooling around up there and fell, and she's rolled down the slope...!!"
"Hanyuu-chaaaan!! Are you okay~?!"
"She rolled very neatly, but... I hope she hasn't sprained anything! Let's go, let's go!!"
Apparently Hanyuu had been playing on the slope and had tumbled all the way down.
There was a road just below.
Mion and the others ran down the steps leading there.
Let's go.
I just hope she isn't hurt...
We ran down to find Tomitake-san and Takano-san there, tending to Hanyuu.
"What in the world happened?! She came rolling right down to me out of nowhere!"
"She rolled down quite cleanly, kukukuku. But it looks like she knocked her head, so she may get a bump later. Best to put something cold on it."
"...Hanyuu, Hanyuu!"
"Can you hear us?! How many fingers am I holding up?!"
Her voice was a little dazed, but it didn't seem like there were sprains or broken bones.
We all breathed a sigh of relief.
She had probably rolled a fair distance, and was just dizzy.
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: "Hau, but I'm so glad. No injuries."
``Well, well! But to tumble down and land right in front of a nurse — that's almost too convenient!``
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: "Oh, when I'm off duty, I'm a bird-watching photographer, you know? Right, Jirou-san?"
"W-well, yes, that's... right! Wahahahaha!"
"Well, if you're a Hinamizawa kid, something like this won't hurt you! Hanyuu's officially a proper Hinamizawa member now."
"Her name is Hanyuu-chan? I don't think I've seen her before. A new friend?"
"Yes, that's right. She only transferred in on Monday."
"This thing... what is it? How unusual. Is it a toy?"
Takano-san reached out and pressed against Hanyuu's horns.
...I supposed they would look unusual as an accessory.
"Ahahahaha, those are Hanyuu-chan's charm point, so you mustn't pull them off."
"Oh, really? I don't think they're very cute myself. ...Because, look —"
``...don't they?``
Suddenly, Hanyuu let out a sound as if she were choking.
"...Takano. Hanyuu is not a monster. If you say it again, I will not forgive you."
"Right, Takano-san — people's... that wasn't kind."
"Ku. I'm sorry — I really didn't mean anything by it."
...Rika-chan's tone was a little sharp.
For Hanyuu, that was no doubt a very wounding word.
changeReason: Translation uses different phrasing that shifts the reader's understanding of the scene.

TextENGNew: Takano-san sometimes said dismissive things without meaning any harm.
But there are things that are all right to say and things that aren't.
Hanyuu stared at Takano-san with a complicated expression.
Seeing that look, Takano-san seemed to realise she might have caused hurt, gave a brief apology, and left with Tomitake-san.
"...Are you all right, Hanyuu? You rolled so neatly that there shouldn't be much injury."
"...Rika... that person just now... au... au..."
"...That person? That's Takano."
"......Takano. ......Takano..."
Hanyuu repeated Takano-san's name as though in a daze.
I began to worry that maybe she had hit her head somewhere strange.
Suddenly, Hanyuu lurched upright and grabbed Rika-chan by the collar.
"R-Rika...!! Au au au!!! It's Takano, Takano! Takano!!!"
"...Yes? That's right, it's Takano."
"No — listen, Rika!!! It's Takano, Takano!!! Don't you remember?!"
"...I know her. Takano Miyo, from the Irie Clinic."
"...You can't... remember...?"
"...Sorry, Hanyuu. What are you talking about...?"
"...R-...Rika......  ...How can this be......"
...In the very last... the very last one... and yet...
Even after enduring the agony of being disembowelled alive to finally burn that name into memory — the name of the true enemy...
changeReason: Substantially different translation that conveys the scene differently.

TextENGNew: ...It hadn't been remembered...
Furude Rika had failed to pass on the memories of the fragment.
